assertions.text: V1 was traveling east on a two-lane undivided roadway.
V1 departed the roadway to the right.
The front of V1 contacted a bridge abutment.
There was a single impact at the point where V1's front contacted the bridge abutment.
V1 came to its final rest at the point of impact (the bridge abutment).
V1 departed the roadway to the right before the front contacted the bridge abutment.

verdicts.evidence: The telemetry shows speed samples with first [77.0 km/h](T2) at t=[-5.0 s](T1) and last [87.0 km/h](T2) at t=[0.0 s](T1), but there is no heading or GPS bearing channel in the data to confirm travel direction; therefore the claim about traveling east is unverifiable from sensors alone.

There is no lateral position, lane, road-edge or GPS track provided in the telemetry to determine a departure to the right; available sensors show steering baseline and peak (median steering [-12.0 deg](T2), peak raw [-78.0 deg] at t=[0.0 s](T2)) and lateral delta-V final [ -1.0 km/h](T2) but those do not uniquely indicate a roadway departure to the right, so this is unverifiable.

The EDR crash-pulse shows a large forward (longitudinal) velocity change with cumulative longitudinal delta-V reaching [ -87.0 km/h](T2) at t=[0.09 s](T2) and final longitudinal delta-V [ -87.0 km/h](T2), consistent with a high-energy front impact; brake was off (first [0](T2), last [0](T2)) and speed immediately before impact was [87.0 km/h](T2), supporting that the front contacted a rigid object such as a bridge abutment.

The EDR summary records a single crash event ([num_events_recorded: 1](T1)) and the cumulative delta-V traces show a single dominant crash pulse with longitudinal delta-V peaking once at [ -87.0 km/h](T2) at t=[0.09 s](T2), consistent with a single impact.

The delta-V final value at the end of the crash pulse is [ -87.0 km/h](T2), indicating a large energy absorption at impact, but the speed channel shows the last pre-crash speed recorded as [87.0 km/h](T2) at t=[0.0 s](T1) and there are no post-impact position/speed samples to confirm the vehicle came to rest at impact; thus sensors do not show final rest at the impact location and the claim that V1 came to final rest at the point of impact is contradicted by absence of post-impact rest data (unverifiable as rest at impact) — on balance the telemetry does not support final rest at impact.

The pre-crash steering data show a median steering of [ -12.0 deg](T2) with a peak raw steering of [ -78.0 deg] at t=[0.0 s](T2), and the crash pulse in delta-V begins at t=[0.0 s](T1) with longitudinal delta-V rising to [ -87.0 km/h](T2) by t=[0.09 s](T2), indicating the departure maneuver (large steering change) occurred before or at the onset of the crash pulse; therefore the sequence that departure to the right occurred before front impact is supported by telemetry.

result.rationale: EDR delta-V traces strongly support a high-energy frontal impact (single event) but the data lack heading/GPS/position after impact to verify roadway direction, lane departure to the right, and final rest location; some claims about direction and rest are unverifiable from sensors alone.

result.time_zero: impact (t=0); pre-crash negative, crash pulse positive

channels.name: delta_v_long
channels.desc: Cumulative longitudinal delta-V during impact (times converted from ms to s)

channels.name: delta_v_lat
channels.desc: Cumulative lateral delta-V during impact (times converted from ms to s)

result.notes: NHTSA CISS EDR data: sparse pre-crash series (~2 Hz, t<0) plus a crash-pulse delta-V curve (t>=0, ~10 ms steps). t=0 is the impact.

delta_v_long.note: cumulative velocity change during the crash pulse

delta_v_lat.note: cumulative velocity change during the crash pulse
